questions.question: According to the lesson, what is the key distinction between digital reach and meaningful impact?

options.text: They are the same thing—more reach always equals more impact
Reach doesn't equal impact; 10,000 superficial views may create less transformation than 10 deep conversations
Impact only happens through in-person connection, never digitally
Digital metrics accurately measure transformation and life change

questions.explanation: Digital platforms can multiply reach 100-1000x compared to in-person approaches—BUT reach doesn't equal impact. Ten thousand superficial views may create less transformation than 10 deep conversations. Quality and depth matter more than metrics. This is why checking analytics obsessively or optimizing purely for viral reach often undermines authentic wisdom-sharing.

questions.question: What is 'strategic vulnerability' in digital wisdom-sharing?

options.text: Sharing only successes and polished moments to maintain professional credibility
Raw-dumping every difficulty and struggle without boundaries
Sharing authentically about struggle and growth while maintaining appropriate boundaries (teaching from scars, not wounds)
Never sharing personal experiences, only abstract principles

questions.explanation: Strategic vulnerability means sharing authentically about struggle, failure, uncertainty, and growth while maintaining appropriate boundaries. It's not oversharing or raw-dumping, nor is it curating only highlight reels. The key questions are: Does this share serve their learning or my need for validation? Am I teaching from the scar (processed, integrated) or the wound (still active, unhealed)? This approach models wholeness—showing successes AND failures, expertise AND ongoing learning.

questions.question: According to research mentioned in the lesson, what percentage of older adults now use social media for connection?

options.text: About 15% of older adults use social media
About 30% of older adults use social media
About 58% of older adults use social media
Nearly all (95%+) older adults use social media

questions.explanation: Research shows 58% of older adults now use social media for meaningful connection. This represents a significant shift in digital engagement among older populations and creates opportunities for digital wisdom-sharing across generations. However, this also means 42% don't use social media, so digital-only approaches exclude a significant portion of older adults. Hybrid approaches (digital + in-person) often work best.

questions.question: What is the evidence grade for digital wisdom-sharing specifically (as distinct from general digital social connection)?

options.text: STRONG - extensive research validates digital wisdom-sharing platforms
MODERATE-HIGH - systematic reviews show consistent benefits
LOW-MODERATE - evidence is limited and emerging, though general digital connection research is stronger
WEAK - no credible research supports digital wisdom-sharing

questions.explanation: The evidence grade for digital wisdom-sharing specifically is LOW-MODERATE. While research on digital platforms for general social connection is MODERATE-HIGH (showing they can reduce loneliness when used intentionally), evidence for wisdom-sharing effectiveness online specifically is limited and emerging. We know video gets 45% higher engagement than text and social media can support connection, but long-term impacts of online legacy work require more research.

questions.question: When does digital wisdom-sharing become harmful and require reassessment or professional support?

options.text: When you receive any negative comments or criticism on your content
When you post consistently on a regular schedule (weekly or biweekly)
When you experience compulsive checking, self-worth tied to metrics, comparison spirals, or neglecting offline relationships
When you use multiple platforms (blog, video, social media) simultaneously

questions.explanation: Digital engagement becomes harmful when you notice: compulsive metric checking, self-worth tied to likes/comments/followers, constant comparison with other creators, performative inauthenticity, or neglecting offline relationships for online presence. These signs indicate unhealthy patterns that may require taking a break, working with a therapist, or re-evaluating whether digital wisdom-sharing serves your wellbeing. Regular posting schedules and multi-platform presence are normal; compulsion and comparison are warning signs.

title: Digital Wisdom Sharing: Platforms and Best Practices - Quiz
